title: Spinning cashmere fibre
subtitle: Drafting the finest natural protein fibre on a supported spindle or a wheel set to low tension
excerpt: Cashmere is the dehaired undercoat of the Cashmere goat at 14 to 16 microns. It drafts very easily, requires a high twist angle to hold the single, and benefits from plying for durability. Blending with fine merino adds wear resistance without sacrificing softness.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from Judith MacKenzie's writing on luxury fibres, general fine-fibre spinning practice, and breed-specific references in 'The Fleece and Fiber Sourcebook' (Robson and Ekarius).

glossaryTerms.definition: Cashmere from which the coarse outer guard hairs have been removed mechanically or by combing, leaving only the fine undercoat fibres.
One thousandth of a millimetre; the standard unit for measuring fibre diameter. Lower micron count equals finer fibre.
The coarse outer coat of an animal such as the Cashmere goat. Guard hairs are scratchy and must be removed before spinning fine yarn.
The angle at which twist wraps around the fibre bundle, measured from the yarn axis. A higher twist angle means more twist and greater strength, but less drape.
Twisting two or more singles together in the opposite direction to the original twist to create a balanced, stronger yarn.
The formation of small fibre balls on a yarn or fabric surface caused by surface fibres tangling together during wear.
A spinning tool held upright in a bowl or dish, with the shaft resting on a support so the spinner can draft with both hands free.

content.text: Cashmere is the fine undercoat combed or shed from the Cashmere goat. At 14 to 16
microns
in diameter, it is finer than superfine merino and softer than any commercially available wool. Commercial cashmere top has already been
dehaired
; raw cashmere straight from the goat still contains
guard hairs
and needs further combing before it is spinnable. Cashmere punishes inconsistency. The fibre slips easily, loses cohesion without enough twist, and
pills
in wear unless the yarn structure is solid. Blending with 20 to 30% fine merino adds durability without removing the characteristic softness.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep hair and clothing back from the drive band, flyer, and bobbin. Tie hair back and roll sleeves before sitting at the wheel.

items.name: Dehaired cashmere top or raw combed cashmere
Fine merino top for blending (18–21 micron)

content.text: Method
Step 1: Prepare raw cashmere if needed

If working from raw cashmere rather than commercial top, comb the fibre on fine-toothed wool combs to remove guard hairs. Load a small amount onto the stationary comb, then draw the moving comb through with light pressure. Guard hairs are coarser and shorter; they collect on the teeth of the moving comb while the fine undercoat transfers cleanly. Repeat until the diz-off produces a consistent, smooth draw. Commercial dehaired top needs only light pre-drafting: strip the braid lengthwise into ribbons 1 to 2 cm wide before spinning.
Step 2: Set up the supported spindle
A
supported spindle

rested in a ceramic bowl gives the most control over cashmere because there is no spindle weight pulling on the gossamer single. Choose a light-whorl spindle (10 to 20 g). Attach a thin leader yarn and park the spindle in the bowl. The spinning hand controls the rate of rotation; the drafting hand holds the fibre supply and advances very gradually.
Step 3: Spin with a controlled short draw

Pinch close to the twist-front with the forward hand to stop twist running into the fibre supply. Draft a short length (2 to 3 cm) with the back hand, then release the pinch and allow twist to enter the drafted zone. The
twist angle
in the finished single should be visible and pronounced: 35 to 45 degrees from vertical. A lower angle means the single lacks the twist to hold together and will break at weak spots during
plying
or in wear.
Step 4: Spin on the wheel (optional)

If using a castle wheel, set the drive band to minimum tension so the take-up force is very light. A high-ratio whorl (12:1 or faster) lets the wheel insert twist quickly before the fibre slips. Use the same short forward draw as on the spindle. Treadle at a steady pace and observe the single as it winds onto the bobbin: consistent diameter means the draft length and treadle speed are matched. Speed up treadling if thin spots appear; slow the draft advance if thick spots develop.
Step 5: Ply for strength

Spin two bobbins or cops of singles, then ply them together in the opposite direction (S-ply if the singles were Z-spun). Plying cashmere singles balances the twist and improves durability significantly. A 2-ply cashmere yarn at 22 to 26 WPI is suitable for shawls and lightweight garments. For a more durable yarn, blend fine merino into the singles at the pre-draft stage: combine equal lengths of cashmere and merino ribbon, draft them together, and spin as one. The merino scale structure grips the cashmere fibres and reduces pilling.
Step 6: Finish the skein

Wind onto the niddy-noddy and count wraps for yardage. Tie in four places with cotton. Soak in 30 °C water with a few drops of wool wash for 20 minutes. Lift out gently, press against the sink wall without wringing, and roll in a towel. Hang with a very light weight and allow to dry away from direct sunlight.

items.cause: Insufficient twist for the fibre diameter
items.fix: Increase wheel ratio or spin the spindle faster. Check the twist angle visually: it should be clearly visible in the single.

items.problem: Fibre supply slips through fingers before twist enters
items.cause: Draft zone too long or pinch hand too relaxed
items.fix: Move the pinch hand closer to the orifice or spindle tip. Shorten the draft length to 2 cm and use a firm but not tight pinch.

items.problem: Finished yarn pills after only a few wears
items.cause: Single under-twisted or yarn not plied
items.fix: Add more twist to the single, ply two singles together, or blend 20 to 30% fine merino into the fibre supply.

items.cause: Guard hairs not fully removed from raw fibre
items.fix: Comb the fibre through fine-toothed combs again. Run a finger across the prepared fibre before spinning: if it catches, more combing is needed.
